channel.title: The Daily Healthcare
channel.description: An 11-episode tour of U.S. healthcare for investors, PMs, analysts, and operators — one domain per episode, from payer economics and Medicare Advantage to RCM, drug pricing, regulation, health IT, and value-based care. Grounded in plain language and the money behind every decision.
channel.itunes:author: Reggie Yang
channel.itunes:summary: An 11-episode tour of U.S. healthcare for investors, PMs, analysts, and operators — one domain per episode, from payer economics to RCM, drug pricing, regulation, health IT, and value-based care.

item.title: Episode 1: Prologue — Orientation
item.description: A grounding tour before the mechanics. Why the U.S. spends $4.5T a year on healthcare and still trails peers on outcomes — the three structural features (consumer isn't the buyer, demand is inelastic, information is asymmetric) that break normal market rules. The five player categories, the ten-step revenue cycle from premium to payment, and the WWII wage-control accident that locked the U.S. into employer-sponsored coverage. Sets up the iron triangle of cost, quality, and access that every later episode comes back to.
item.itunes:summary: Why the U.S. spends $4.5T on healthcare and trails on outcomes — and the WWII accident that locked it in.

item.title: Episode 2: Part 1 — Payer & Insurance
item.description: The payer universe end-to-end. Risk pooling, plan design (HMO/PPO/EPO/POS), and the fully-insured vs. self-insured split that covers two-thirds of commercially insured Americans. All four parts of Medicare plus Medigap, Medicaid and the dual-eligible population, TRICARE/VA, and the ACA marketplace. The operational layer — eligibility, prior auth, coordination of benefits, payer-provider contracting — and the economics: the MLR floor that shapes payer strategy, the underwriting cycle, IBNR reserves, and the employer point-solutions ecosystem layered on top of every medical plan.
item.itunes:summary: The payer universe — from risk pooling to Medicare Advantage to the MLR rule that shaped UnitedHealth's empire.

item.title: Episode 3: Part 2 — Provider & Clinical Operations
item.description: Where care actually happens and who pays for it. Hospitals, ambulatory surgery centers, physician groups, FQHCs, and the site-of-service differentials driving hospital acquisition of practices. The patient journey, clinical documentation as the engine of billing and legal defense, care-team roles, and the workforce crisis driving burnout and staffing-agency economics. Post-acute care, long-term care and the Medicaid spend-down, behavioral health, telehealth, and digital-health business models. Consolidation, PE roll-ups, and how to read a hospital Form 990 or payer 10-K for what the footnotes are really saying.
item.itunes:summary: Hospitals, ASCs, PE roll-ups, and the site-of-service arbitrage reshaping where U.S. care actually happens.

item.title: Episode 4: Part 3 — Revenue Cycle Management
item.description: The $100B+ industry that exists because getting paid for care is so hard. The revenue cycle end-to-end: patient access and eligibility on the front, ICD-10/CPT/HCPCS coding in the middle, and DRG/APC grouping that turns codes into dollars under IPPS, OPPS, and the Medicare physician fee schedule. The chargemaster, UB-04 vs. CMS-1500 forms, EDI transactions (837/835/270/271), claim scrubbing, adjudication, denial management, and appeals. The patient as "last payer," the No Surprises Act, and the IDR process that's now the real battleground for out-of-network billing.
item.itunes:summary: $250–300B a year gets spent just on billing for healthcare — here's the ten-step relay that explains why.

item.title: Episode 5: Part 4 — Care Models & Payment Reform
item.description: The slow migration from fee-for-service to paying for outcomes. The full value-based care spectrum from pay-for-reporting to global budgets, and the mechanics of the big models: ACOs and MSSP tracks, bundled payments, capitation and global budgets, MIPS, HEDIS, and Star Ratings. Risk adjustment and HCC coding as the financial engine behind Medicare Advantage. The VBC-enabler sub-sector (Oak Street, ChenMed, Agilon, Privia, Aledade) that ran hot on MA full-risk economics and is now being repriced as v28 and rate pressure expose who can actually manage cost vs. who was just coding harder.
item.itunes:summary: Volume vs. value — the 20-year drift from fee-for-service, and the MA full-risk sub-sector now being repriced.

item.title: Episode 6: Part 5 — Pharmacy, Devices & Diagnostics
item.description: The product economy beneath the clinical encounter. Drug development from bench to bedside, FDA approval pathways (NDA/ANDA/BLA/510(k)/PMA), and the brand-generic-biosimilar pricing cliff. The alphabet of drug pricing (AWP, WAC, ASP), the 340B program and contract-pharmacy controversies, and the PBM layer (Caremark, Express Scripts, OptumRx) being reshaped by the IRA's Part D redesign and bipartisan rebate reform. Retail, specialty, and mail-order pharmacy economics. GLP-1s as the single largest cost story in employer plans. Medical devices (510(k) vs. PMA) and the diagnostics duopoly (LabCorp/Quest) with its own reimbursement rules.
item.itunes:summary: Pharma, devices, and diagnostics — 340B, PBMs, GLP-1s, and the pricing rules behind each product layer.

item.title: Episode 7: Part 6 — Regulatory & Compliance
item.description: The regulatory stack that makes healthcare the most rule-bound industry in the U.S. economy. CMS as the invisible kingmaker and the rulemaking calendar investors should track like earnings. HIPAA's privacy, security, and breach-notification rules. The fraud-and-abuse trio — Stark self-referral, the Anti-Kickback Statute, and the False Claims Act with its qui tam whistleblower engine behind most billion-dollar healthcare settlements. EMTALA, FDA jurisdiction across drugs/devices/software, state-level CON laws, accreditation (Joint Commission, NCQA, URAC), and the seven elements of a corporate compliance program.
item.itunes:summary: Stark, AKS, False Claims Act, HIPAA, EMTALA — the five rules that shape every healthcare business decision.

item.title: Episode 8: Part 7 — Health IT & Data Standards
item.description: The digital plumbing every clinical and billing workflow runs on. The $36B HITECH investment that put an EHR in every clinic, the vendor landscape dominated by Epic, and the data standards — HL7 v2 still running most interfaces, FHIR as the interoperability bet, X12 for EDI, plus the terminology stack (ICD-10, CPT, SNOMED, LOINC, RxNorm, NDC). Health information exchanges and TEFCA, clinical decision support, healthcare analytics, and the current AI wave from ambient documentation to imaging. Cybersecurity — why healthcare is the #1 ransomware target — and the 21st Century Cures Act's information-blocking rules.
item.itunes:summary: Epic, FHIR, HIPAA, and the $36B HITECH investment that turned healthcare IT into a strategic layer.

item.title: Episode 9: Part 8 — Population Health & Public Health
item.description: Zooming out from the individual encounter to the population. The difference between population health (a health system's defined group) and public health (government infrastructure), and the upstream vs. downstream intervention debate. Social determinants, the five domains, and why zip code predicts health better than genetic code. Chronic disease and the 80/20 spending rule. Preventive care, health equity and disparities measurement, the basics of epidemiology without the math, CDC and state/local public health capacity, community health workers, and the mental health and substance use crisis at population scale.
item.itunes:summary: Population and public health — SDOH, the 80/20 rule, and why zip code predicts health better than genetic code.

item.title: Episode 10: Part 9 — Patient Experience & Access
item.description: The front door where patients actually meet the system. Consumerism, high-deductible plans, and the HSA/FSA/HRA toolkit shaping employer-sponsored coverage. The health-literacy gap (only 12% of adults are proficient), patient portals as the digital front door, the hospital and Transparency in Coverage rules, and why transparency alone doesn't bend cost. Rising medical debt, CAHPS and HCAHPS tied to reimbursement, and the convergence with patient safety measurement. PROMs and PREMs as the "what patients actually report" layer. Access barriers across geography, language, disability, and the digital divide, plus the invisible family caregiver workforce.
item.itunes:summary: HDHPs, health literacy, price transparency, medical debt — the consumer side of a system not designed for consumers.

item.title: Episode 11: Epilogue — Synthesis & What's Next
item.description: Pulling the nine domains back together. The whole system is one connected graph: a payer decision reshapes provider economics, runs through RCM, is gated by regulation, powered by IT, and lands on a patient. A look back at the ten-step dollar flow with the full context now loaded in, and a look forward at the forces actively reshaping the system — AI in clinical and administrative workflows, pharmacy disruption, the next phase of payment-model reform, equity as a strategic priority, workforce transformation, and the consumerization of care.
item.itunes:summary: The nine domains aren't silos — they're a graph. Final synthesis and what's shaping the next five years.
